text.title: ModelComparison — Multi-Model Benchmark Runner
text.summary: ModelComparison class: running identical test sets across multiple models, ranking by metric, latency comparison, and parallelization for large test sets.

rule.statement: Run all models at temperature=0 with the identical system prompt and test cases.
rule.rationale: Any difference in prompt or temperature produces incomparable results; the comparison must control everything except the model.

rule.statement: Use at least 50 cases per task type for ModelComparison; below 50, metric rankings reverse between runs due to sampling variance.
rule.rationale: Statistical reliability requires sufficient sample size; fewer than 50 cases produce confidence intervals too wide for actionable decisions.

rule.statement: Parallelize model evaluation with asyncio.gather or separate subagents — never run models sequentially for large test sets.
rule.rationale: 3 models x 100 cases x 1s latency = 300s sequential; parallelization reduces this to ~100s wall-clock time.

rule.statement: When using ModelComparison rankings for model selection, invert the latency ranking — lower latency is better, but the default ranking sorts higher values first.
rule.rationale: The comparison report assumes "higher is better" for all metrics. Latency ranking must be explicitly inverted or the fastest model is ranked last.

antipattern.description: Using ModelComparison.rankings["latency_ms"][0] as the "best" model selection.
antipattern.reason: The highest latency model is ranked first by the default sort; the agent selects the slowest model when it intends to pick the fastest.

rule.statement: Maintain a frozen "golden" test set for regression detection and a separate "current" test set that grows with new failure modes from production.
rule.rationale: A growing test set captures new edge cases; a frozen golden set ensures regressions are detectable even as coverage expands.

rule.statement: Store benchmark datasets in version control (Git LFS or DVC) alongside the prompt versions they were designed to test.
rule.rationale: Changes to either the dataset or the prompt should trigger a benchmark re-run; version co-location makes this dependency explicit.

antipattern.description: Using the same test set to tune prompts and then to evaluate quality.
antipattern.reason: The test set is no longer an unbiased evaluator once it has been used to optimize — benchmark overfitting produces inflated scores.
